chunks.text: Might be worth it. Come to you. Please take a seat. Thanks so much.

Hey, great to have you here. Good morning. Welcome to the Health Committee. Just advise you around the rules of Parliament mean that when hearing your submission, we have to be careful not to hear allegations that may seriously damage a person's reputation. If we're concerned around that, we'll jump in and ask you to move to your next point.

But look, good to have you here. I know you're very keen to submit on this bill, and we have you for 10 minutes. There are likely to be questions. But welcome to the Health Committee. Sarah, you're Doctor... Yasek. Dr. Yasek. Welcome. I'm Sam. You'll know members around the committee as well.

We have you for 10 minutes. If you want to leave us time to ask questions, you may, but that time is yours, and I'll hand it to you now. Thank you. Absolutely. Thank you. Kia ora, Sam and Health Select Committee. Good to see you all again, and thank you for giving us this time today. So in 1960, this Parliament removed attempted suicide from criminal law. The then Health Minister who introduced that change was Rex Mason, one of the great law reformers of last century.

And he told the House almost 70 years ago that it was better not to affix the stigma of criminal law upon someone in crisis. Both sides of the house agreed. We are asking you to apply that very same insight to a different issue today, accidental drug overdose.

That when someone is at their most vulnerable, the shadow of the criminal law doesn't protect them, it keeps them from reaching for help. It has struck me as I've spoken to families impacted by fatal drug overdose over the years and in reading the submissions to this bill, how the current legal settings that had a hand in the death of their loved one also cast a shadow of stigma and has added an unthinkable burden to their suffering.

I want to thank the committee for allowing anonymous submissions to this bill, which has at least provided a vehicle for those loved ones and those directly impacted by non-fatal overdose to at last be heard. But it's not just stigma that this bill will impact. It will very directly save lives. We, of course, support this bill, and of the 256 submissions that have been made public so far, only one was in opposition. The rest are in support.

Of those, there are a handful of submissions from experts, and then the lion's share are from people who have been impacted in some way by the current legislative settings. This is because the impacted community know very, very well, that in reality, the fear of criminalization and the hesitation it creates are costing lives.

We spend a lot of money trying to save people's lives from a variety of other causes, from drownings to road accidents. But in this case, all we are asking for is to pass a piece of legislation that will enable equal access to emergency care. The stories in the anonymous submissions and the stories I've heard from people paint a picture of why the law is needed.

A number of bereaved families that I've spoken to have heartbreak stories of loved ones that have been left achingly near to help. Outside an emergency department on the lawn of a neighbor. The intention of getting help was clearly there, but it is clear from these stories that the fear of consequences prevented them from properly accessing it, and those people are no longer with us today as a result.

Police in our country are great at working alongside harm reduction efforts, and we believe that this law will help to protect police by providing a clear framework that removes the burden on frontline officers from having to make difficult decisions on the fly, and also protect them from being accused of wrongdoing. Thank you.

We've made five recommendations in our written submission that I just want to really briefly touch on and then hand over to you. The first is to explicitly protect against warrantless arrest, which is a relatively minor change, but we note in US states where they do have this additional protection, there are demonstrably better outcomes.

Last year, the Supreme Court of Canada had a ruling that found that arrest protection is essential to this life-saving purpose of the legislation. Secondly, and for clarity, to avoid police potentially having to assess everybody on scene, we recommend removing remains to offer support or assistance.

This would just ensure protection in the chaos of a rapidly moving situation. Thirdly, we think that probably the intention of the draft is to cover all illicit substances, so to extend to substances covered by the Psychoactive Substances Act and the Medicines Act and the corresponding offenses to remove ambiguity.

Fourthly, we recommend some strengthening of the social sharing provisions, and fifthly, removing some redundant words that add to legal complexity in the bill. This bill, while new to New Zealand, is an extraordinarily mainstream measure and a no-brainer. Every single US state has a version of Good Samaritan provision in place, whether red or blue. And by passing this bill, you will help to prevent serious health harm, unnecessary loss of life, and the grief and suffering that goes along with it.

And thank you for your time. Thank you, Sarah. Do members have que-- Cam. Cheers. Thanks for your submission. There's quite a bit of data in here about the new submission. But I couldn't find anything talking about how many prosecutions there've been since the guidelines were changed.

So there's the perceived risk and the real risk of prosecution. This bill is trying to change the perceived risk up front, so people at front of mind call for help. But how many people have actually been prosecuted who are in a social sharing environment? It's not something we have focused heavily on because the perceived risk of prosecution is indeed the bit that's causing the problems. Prosecutions do happen, but we don't wish to persecute the police for prosecuting people, I guess.

Do you have anything to add, Jasit? Yeah. There's currently no such distinction in the law for social sharing, so the data is not available that would describe those specific scenarios. What we've seen since the discretion amendment, because I think you're referring to this one, we've had numbers of prosecutions, including charges and convictions last year, that exceeded the pre-amendment numbers.

Mm. So the number of prosecutions has been rising, and it looks like that amendment hasn't been that effective in preventing those possession prosecutions. Okay. Thank you. Maria. Just a quick question around your recommendation on warrantless arrests. Is it the issue of a warrantless arrest, or is it arrest in general that you're wanting protection from?

That would be general. Yeah, that would be general. The warrantless arrest would be probably the most common situation in those scenarios. But yeah, that would be in general all arrests for the specified offenses. Yeah. It's hard to imagine. I guess maybe police came into a situation where they already had a warrant or something, and then they- Yeah... found an overdose situation. But the distinction is just not to have an arrest in that- That's essentially right. Yes.

Okay. So I can-- Thank you. Any other questions from members? Sorry, Chair. Can I ask a question? Sorry. How easy to identify what is social sharing or small drug sales or dealing? You go, Jasit. Yeah. That's probably a bit of an arbitrary issue.

So at the moment, the bill specifies it with certain conditions. So there is a limit of people with whom this is shared. In our submission, we raised a few ideas about how to make it more tight and how to better reflect the real-life scenarios. But in essence, what we want to avoid is people being prosecuted for very common scenarios.

So for example, a group of friends are going to a party. One of the people has a contact somewhere and is able to kind of procure drugs on behalf of the group, and then later shares that. Technically now, for Class B substances, for example, for MDMA, that would be an offense of supply. And we would like that to be in that protected class of offenses when this is clearly not commercial supply.

This is clearly not a situation of somebody dealing drugs to friends, but it's just a group of friends getting it together, and people being very concerned that, hey, if we call, that person may be at risk. Yeah. But do you think that's practical? Because as far as I know, most of the drug use or drug dealer, they are a small dealer. So normally they sell drugs within their friends group rather than they sell to strangers.

So maybe a group of 50, 30 people having a party, and then one of them actually starts selling drugs in there. We can actually call them social sharing as well. How can you identify that? But that is not helping us or to stop people taking drugs.

Yeah. So that only applies in those emergency scenarios. So there is no way that the police would otherwise discover that from happening. So there would be no prosecutions, because the police would never discover that anyway. So because these protections only apply in the situation where emergency help is called, then in all the other scenarios, that continues to be an offense, whether it's social sharing or whether it's kind of larger scale supply.

That distinction only applies for in emergency scenarios. It's not going to significantly impede police prosecuting people. So does mean all the- It's more focused on that very real situation where-- Because normally when somebody's taking a substance- Of course... somebody will have got it for everybody. Just one more. No, no. I'm over time.

Sure. So I'm going to have to go. I just wanted to examine with the police element and what you might know or have engaged with the police around police training, around how they exercise discretion. Do you have any insights in terms of how our police are trained to exercise discretion on this issue?

Yes. And it doesn't heavily impact this bill, I would say. So discretion, we have had a couple of conversations. I think part of the instruction around discretion was to not apply it if there was anything else you could charge the person for. And I suspect that's why we've seen an increase.

We've had more policing, and therefore more people being swept up in that, and drug charges being part of it. That's what we can sort of garner from the information. But it doesn't mean that that's the full picture either. So yeah. Thank you. Thank you. Thanks, Sarah. Thank you.

Thank you both for making yourself available. Your work in this space. Thank you so much. Thank you. Right. And now I'm at the New Zealand Law Society. Debbie coming here by Zoom. They'll be coming in. Chair, I have to be in the house to be in the house. I've just done my Hello. My camera doesn't seem to be working, I'm afraid.

Jeremy Finn here. My camera doesn't seem to be working, so... Right. I've got Professor Jeremy Finn and, Debbie Wilson as well joining us today. Jeremy, we can't see you, but we can definitely hear you. Well, thank you for that. I'm sorry, I don't know what the malfunction is.

But, the Law Society, to appear with Professor Wilson on this, the Law Society doesn't really want to take your time talking about policy issues. But we do have, and have raised in our submission, a number of issues about the drafting, which may cause more complexity than I think has been expected by the people, the proponents of the bill, and which might cause it not to work as it was intended.

I wanted to talk first about the Search and Surveillance Act issues and 34B4, and then I will hand over to Professor Wilson to talk about 34B1B. And if there are time, we can talk about other matters later. Obviously, it's a wide-ranging submission, and we would expect members to have questions, and we want to leave time for that.

The search provisions in 34B subsection iv state that to the extent that in the circumstances described in subsection iiA a constable has reasonable grounds to believe, et cetera, the constable must be treated as not having the grounds that would authorize a search.

Now, there's a bit of a problem here because, the question at the moment the bill only applies to warrantless searches. I understand from the previous speaker that they were concerned also about searches with a warrant, and that's a point which must be made clear in the legislation.

Whichever way the parliament goes, it's not satisfactory to leave the question of searches with a warrant so that we're hanging in the air. The bill should say which it is. The second problem is that it is not clear on the wording what will happen where the constable already has grounds to believe that an offense was being committed and could therefore make an arrest or could perform a warrantless search, or could use it to procure a warrant.

So suppose we have an undercover constable at a festival concert, sees somebody exchanging pills for money, and then a customer or the dealer themselves collapses. Now, the fact that the person was handing out pills in exchange for money would be a basis for getting a warrant to search their own house.

It would give a basis for an arrest. Do we then say because somebody collapsed, that they can't use that knowledge to procure a warrant or indeed to make an arrest then or later? What would happen if there'd been a warrant already issued on the basis of the constable's reasonable belief and the facts they knew, and then before the warrant is executed, the emergency situation comes up? Now, I know they're fairly technical issues, but you're dealing here with the difference between people who may be tried and found guilty of offenses and spend a lot of time in

prison and those who don't. And the legislation should be, we suggest, very clear. It would be particularly important, we think, to make a specific provision for cases where about information gathered during a emergency call, whether to be used for a application for a search warrant or not. As it stands at the moment, they can't use it for the purpose of a warrantless search, but it's not clear whether they could use it for a warranted one.

There's a related issue about how this will work where the social sharing exception is in place because the policing that will often require the police to actually have inspected the premises where the emergency happens to see whether or not there are more plants or not. And the question of whether they can use that knowledge is relevant, or is it a case that the person charged would have to show that the social sharing exception applied. These are technical issues, I know, and the Law Society would be very happy to further discuss them

with the drafters or the committee if need be. But I'll hand over there to Professor Wilson. Thank you. I'm going to just talk a little bit about the definition in subsection 5 of drug related acute adverse reactions, because this is obviously quite central to what the amendment means to do.

There's a couple of wording in there that seems a bit strange. So it starts off by saying that a drug related acute adverse reaction means an unwanted or harmful physical or psychological reaction. So the language of unwanted, we're not quite sure who it is that would determine that. So is it the person calling for help?

Do they have to decide that it was unwanted, or do they actually have to ask the person who's taken the drugs whether the reaction is something that they wanted or not? And that's obviously going to be problematic because that person is in distress and perhaps unconscious. So how do you work out what unwanted actually means? The alternative is that it's a harmful physical or psychological reaction.

And again, we sort of looked at that and we thought, well, if you're talking about taking a controlled drug, isn't the point that every reaction is harmful in some way? So we're not quite sure what those words are trying to achieve, but they could be quite confusing to apply in practice. The second part of the definition, if you continue on, under subsection C, it requires that a reasonable person would believe that the individual requires emergency assistance.

In law, when we talk about the reasonable person test, that's sort of an average person, and that's problematic in this kind of situation because the person who's going to be considering whether emergency assistance is required is probably also drunk or on drugs. So that means they're slightly less competent. They're not operating at the standard of an average person.

In our submission, we talk about cases in Canada and in New Zealand that have looked at this, and they find it quite difficult to apply a reasonable person test to a situation where somebody is affected by drugs or alcohol. And they ask the question of what the reasonable person actually is in that circumstance. So is it just the reasonable, average person that's stumbled past the scene and is sober, or is it a reasonable person who's on drugs?

So that's kind of a lower standard because they're not completely competent. Or do you go the other way and say that actually it's a higher standard, it's a reasonable drug user who is familiar enough with drugs that they would actually understand whether this was a really bad reaction or whether it was just the reaction that the person was aiming for.

So we're suggesting that maybe the language needs to be considered there, because it's not completely clear what the test is actually going to be. If we've still got time left, I'm going to pass back to Jeremy to talk about accessories after the fact. I think if, through you, Mr. Chairman, we were just open for questions now. We know your time is limited.

Excellent. Thank you. We have two and a half minutes to go. Are there questions from members? Yeah. Hamish. Excellent. Thank you. So I suppose the general question is, I appreciate that you're generally supportive of the bill. You would say with improvement to the drafting, that you would support the bill even more?

I think the position we have, well, the Law Society hasn't taken it to its membership, but any bill that tries to save life is generally a good thing. So we start from that premise. The question of where you draw the line between measures which promote life but may mean people avoid criminal liability is very much a question for Parliament. It's not for us. What we're saying at the moment, this bill will not quite, we think, achieve what it sets out to do.

Yeah, and I suppose I probably worded that question poorly. Do you think with a few amendments, this bill would be able to achieve its goals, its objective goals, or do you think that actually there's fundamentally flaws for it to reach its objective goals? I would say if it can be amended appropriately, it can work, and could work very well. I heard some of the previous speaker talk about the fact that America has versions of this, and they seem to be working. So it's a matter of getting it right, not a matter of whether you should do it at all.

Thank you. Okay. All right. There do not appear to be any other questions from members. Thank you very much for your submission. Appreciate it. Thank you for the opportunity to appear. Thank you. Thank you. All right. We will move back into private session
